our_argument_summary: Earth's hemispheric energy balance is remarkably symmetric (Stephens et al. 2012). The +0.34 W/m² figure the dome cites is a decadal trend in Earth's energy imbalance (Raghuraman et al. 2025, PNAS), driven by aerosol changes, Arctic ice loss, and cloud shifts — not a static hemispheric difference. The dome's disc geometry is inherently asymmetric but provides no radiation budget calculation translating geometry to a W/m² prediction. Verdict: Std Model Explains.

relevant_files: raw-text/ (no monitor.py or pull_data.py found in extracted dome source)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline exists for WIN-066. No energy budget scripts, CERES data fetching, or hemispheric comparison logic.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Not applicable — no validation exists for this WIN.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None. The dome does not invoke aetheric refraction for energy budget claims.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — our code_analysis tags correctly show monitoring=none.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No dome monitoring code references WIN-066, hemispheric energy, heat asymmetry, or CERES data. Zero automated validation exists for this WIN. Confirmed by searching all extracted dome source files.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Hemispheric energy budget is quantified by CERES satellite data (Stephens 2012, Loeb 2018, Raghuraman 2025). The dome's contribution is claiming the observation 'confirms dome asymmetry' without changing any numerical prediction.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Hemispheric energy studies predate the dome model by decades (Trenberth 1997, Stephens 2012). The +0.34 W/m² figure from Raghuraman et al. (2025) was published before dome V51.0 (2026). Post-hoc adoption.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No dome formula derives any W/m² value from dome parameters. The dome site says the Ovoid Tilt geometry 'predicts' NH energy excess but provides no radiation integral, no flux-vs-radius calculation, and no numerical derivation.

holes_found.description: MISSING KILLER ARGUMENT — dome geometry over-predicts by factor of ~274. A flat disc with sun at 5,733 km altitude creates extreme inverse-square flux falloff. Computing the integrated solar energy over the inner half (NH) vs outer half (SH) of a 20,015 km disc gives a ~2.3:1 ratio — roughly 93 W/m² NH excess, not 0.34 W/m². If the dome DID compute its radiation budget, it would wildly over-predict the asymmetry. This is a stronger argument than 'no derivation exists' because it shows the derivation would FAIL.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence to detail_evidence: 'Moreover, a flat-disc geometry with a local sun at 5,733 km altitude produces extreme inverse-square flux falloff — the inner half of the disc receives roughly 2.3× the energy of the outer half, predicting a ~93 W/m² hemispheric difference (factor of ~270× larger than observed). The dome geometry doesn't just lack a derivation; the calculation would contradict the observation.'

holes_found.description: DOME CLAIM MISREPRESENTATION in detail_claim. The dome's wins.html says the Ovoid Tilt geometry 'predicts a persistent Northern Hemisphere energy absorption excess' — framing +0.34 W/m² as a STATIC difference. Our detail_claim says 'emerging +0.34 W/m²/decade trend in absorbed solar radiation.' These describe different physical quantities. The dome says static excess; reality is a trend. Our review should be precise about what the dome claims vs what the observation actually is.
holes_found.recommendation: Revise detail_claim to match what the dome actually says: 'Dome predicts persistent Northern Hemisphere energy absorption excess of +0.34 W/m², claimed to confirm dome geometric asymmetry.' Then in the evidence, note that the real observation is a decadal trend, not a static feature — making the dome's framing doubly wrong.

holes_found.description: VERDICT TEXT cites 'Stephens & L'Ecuyer (2015)' without a DOI link, while the evidence section links to Stephens et al. (2012) with doi:10.1038/ngeo1580. These are different papers (2012 is Nature Geoscience; 2015 is Surveys in Geophysics, doi:10.1007/s10712-015-9340-1). Both are valid references, but the unlinkable 2015 citation in the verdict text looks inconsistent with the linked 2012 citation in the evidence. A pedantic dome defender could claim we're conflating sources.
holes_found.recommendation: Either add a DOI link for the 2015 paper in the verdict text, or consolidate on one reference. Since the 2012 paper is already linked in the evidence, consider changing the verdict text to reference 'Stephens et al. (2012)' for consistency, or link both.

holes_found.description: UNVERIFIABLE PNAS DOI. The DOI 10.1073/pnas.2511595122 for Raghuraman et al. (2025, PNAS) returned a 403 error when I attempted to verify. The DOI format is consistent with PNAS but the paper content cannot be independently confirmed. The Cycle 1 review flagged that the +0.34 W/m² figure needs careful sourcing — if this DOI is incorrect, we've repeated the citation error pattern from Cycle 1.
holes_found.recommendation: Manually verify that doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122 resolves to the correct paper. If the paper discusses Earth's energy imbalance trend (not hemispheric asymmetry specifically), clarify the distinction in our text.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's flat-disc geometry would predict ~93 W/m² NH-SH difference (2.3:1 ratio from inverse-square law on a flat disc with local sun), not 0.34 W/m². This is 274× the observed value. The dome doesn't just lack a derivation — the derivation would refute the claim. This transforms the argument from 'no prediction' to 'wrong prediction.'
sources: Computed: integrate solar flux (h/(r²+h²)^1.5 × 2πr dr) over inner vs outer halves of disc with h=5733 km, R=20015 km

stronger_arguments.description: Cross-reference with WIN-067 (Antarctic gravity hole): the same dome geometry that predicts 90% gravity drop at the rim also predicts extreme energy drop at the rim. Both expose the fundamental problem that the dome's disc geometry creates extreme center-to-rim gradients that don't exist in reality. The gravity gradient (WIN-067: g drops 90%) and energy gradient (WIN-066: 2.3:1 ratio) are the same geometric effect — inverse-square/distance falloff on a flat disc.
sources: WIN-067 detail_finding: 'Dome predicts 90% gravity drop at rim'

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's solar model already borrows globe parameters (WIN-056: uses 23.45° axial tilt). Any seasonal variation in the dome's solar orbit radius that makes hemispheric energy closer to symmetric would be derived from globe obliquity — the dome would need to explain asymmetry using a model that silently imports globe symmetry.
sources: WIN-056: 'Uses globe's declination formula (23.45° axial tilt)'

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies a real asymmetry in Earth's energy budget. The observation is genuine: CERES data show a small but real hemispheric energy difference, and there IS an emerging trend in Earth's energy imbalance. The dome is also correct that its geometry is inherently asymmetric — the NP-centered disc does receive different solar flux at different radii.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three independent problems: (1) No derivation connects dome geometry to +0.34 W/m² — it's pure post-hoc adoption; (2) If the derivation were attempted, inverse-square falloff on the dome disc predicts ~93 W/m² (274× too large); (3) The dome's static geometry cannot explain a temporal trend (the +0.34 W/m² is a decadal rate of change, driven by aerosol, ice, and cloud changes that the dome model has no framework to address).

deeper_analysis: Cycle 2 assessment: The text was substantially rewritten since Cycle 1 (evidence doubled from 413→872 chars, verdict +49%). All three critical/major issues from Cycle 1 were fixed: (1) DOI corrected from the fabricated ngeo2516 to the correct ngeo1580 for Stephens 2012; (2) +0.34 W/m² correctly identified as a decadal trend rather than static difference; (3) Stephens' hemispheric symmetry finding properly acknowledged. The geometric asymmetry acknowledgment was added. The text is now scientifically sound in its core argument. However, Cycle 2 reveals a significant missed opportunity: the dome's own geometry, if computed, would OVER-predict hemispheric energy asymmetry by a factor of ~274 (inverse-square flux falloff on a flat disc). This transforms a 'no prediction' argument into a 'wrong prediction' argument — much stronger. Additionally, the detail_claim should more accurately represent what the dome says (persistent static excess) vs what the data show (emerging trend). The verdict 'Std Model Explains' remains correct — the observation is well-explained by standard climate science, and the dome has neither a derivation nor a plausible mechanism.

citations_verified: Stephens et al. (2012, Nature Geoscience) doi:10.1038/ngeo1580 — DOI format correct for Nature Geoscience; this is the well-known 'An update on Earth's energy balance in light of the latest global observations.' DOI redirect returned 303 (paywalled) but the DOI-to-article mapping is correct.
citations_unchecked: Raghuraman et al. (2025, PNAS) doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122 — DOI returned 403 error. Cannot verify paper title or content. Format is consistent with PNAS. The Cycle 1 review recommended this DOI as the correct source for the +0.34 W/m²/decade figure, but independent verification was not possible.
Stephens & L'Ecuyer (2015) — Referenced in verdict text without DOI link. Likely 'The Earth's energy balance' in Surveys in Geophysics (doi:10.1007/s10712-015-9340-1). Not linkable in current text.
